name: Hoderi
description: Hoderi, also known as Hoori, is a significant figure in Japanese mythology, primarily known for his role in the lineage of the Imperial family. He is regarded as the elder brother of Hoori (also known as Hikohaho-demi or Yamasachi-hiko), the ancestor of Emperor Jimmu. Hoderi is often associated with the sea and fishing, contrasting with his brother who is linked to hunting. The mythic narrative surrounding Hoderi revolves around the loss of Hoori's fishhook and the subsequent quest to retrieve it, leading to interactions with the sea god Watatsumi. This episode highlights themes of sibling rivalry, cosmic order, and the divine origins of the Japanese imperial line. Hoderi's eventual submission to his younger brother Hoori is interpreted as a symbolic representation of the subjugation of earlier, perhaps indigenous, powers to the Yamato clan, who would eventually establish the imperial dynasty. He is an important, if sometimes overlooked, figure in understanding the complex tapestry of Japanese creation myths and ancestral narratives.
subtitle: Elder Brother of the Imperial Ancestor

keyMyths.title: The Lost Fishhook
keyMyths.description: Hoori borrows his brother Hoderi's fishhook but loses it in the sea. Hoderi demands its return, leading Hoori to descend to the sea floor and seek the help of the sea god Watatsumi. Watatsumi helps Hoori find the hook and provides him with magical jewels to control the tides, ultimately allowing Hoori to overcome Hoderi. This myth establishes the divine lineage of the imperial family and symbolizes the subjugation of the sea to the land.

keyMyths.title: Meeting Princess Toyotama
keyMyths.description: While in the underwater palace of Watatsumi, Hoori meets and marries Princess Toyotama, the sea god's daughter. They live together for several years, during which time Hoori possesses the power and knowledge of the sea. This union further solidifies the connection between the imperial line and the divine realm, specifically the realm of the sea.

keyMyths.title: Hoderi's Submission
keyMyths.description: After Hoori returns to land with the magical jewels, he uses them to subdue Hoderi. Hoderi acknowledges Hoori's superiority and pledges allegiance. This event is interpreted as the symbolic submission of the sea people (or earlier inhabitants of Japan) to the Yamato clan, the ancestors of the imperial family. The subjugation reinforces the legitimacy of the imperial rule.

keyMyths.title: Ancestor of the Hayato
keyMyths.description: Following his submission, Hoderi is said to have become the ancestor of the Hayato people, a group known for their distinctive culture and often associated with the southern regions of Japan. This connection suggests a complex relationship between the imperial court and regional powers, with Hoderi representing a lineage that was incorporated into the broader imperial narrative.
keyMyths.source: Various Shinto Shrines and Local Legends
